$comment: Collision behavior: what happens when the skill-supplied request ALREADY contains the field the strategy will inject. Two distinct outcomes the spec pins: HEADER and BODY injections OVERWRITE the skill-supplied value (vault-injected value wins, prevents skill from controlling the credential field); QUERY-PARAM injection APPENDS (HTTP allows duplicate query keys; last-write-wins on most servers, but two distinct ?key= entries are visibly anomalous in receipt-side audit). These are not adversarial vectors — a well-behaved skill should never set these fields — but they pin the behavior so impls don't silently disagree.

vectors.name: header_overwrite_authorization_bearer
vectors.description: Skill supplied its own Authorization header (a misconfigured skill, perhaps with a stale token). The vault MUST overwrite with the credential it injects — the skill never sees, and never controls, the credential field.

vectors.name: query_append_duplicate_key
vectors.description: Skill already supplied ?key=skill-was-here; queryParam strategy APPENDS &key=<credential> rather than overwriting. The duplicate-key on the upstream URL is a visible audit anomaly — operators see the receipt's wall_clock_ms + upstream_status, and a savvy reviewer notices two ?key= entries in the upstream's access log. The behavior is INTENTIONAL — the proxy doesn't silently mutate the skill's query string. Operators MUST configure queryParam only for services where the skill itself does not set the same param.

vectors.name: body_overwrite_at_json_path
vectors.description: Skill JSON body already contains the credential field. The bodyField strategy OVERWRITES — same rationale as the header case. The output is re-serialized canonically (sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':')), so the field order is alphabetical regardless of input order.
